polymorphic-collections.label: Polymorphic Collections
polymorphic-collections.summary: Use one shared method call on different child objects, so a mixed list can be handled with one clean loop.

sketch0.title: Put different subclasses in one list

sketch1.title: Call the shared method in a loop

sketch2.title: Move the loop into a helper

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch0.title: Create a mixed catalog list
try_polymorphic_collections_sketch0.prompt: The lesson used delivery objects. Now practice with catalog media. In `main.py`, create a mixed `catalog` list with `Book("A Wrinkle in Time")` and `Movie("Up")`. Do not print yet; this step is only about building one list that holds different child objects.
try_polymorphic_collections_sketch0.hint: A Python list can hold different object types. Put the Book object and Movie object inside the same list.

help.concept: Polymorphism starts with a shared interface. Book and Movie are different classes, but both support `label()`.
help.hint_1: Put both objects inside one list.
help.hint_2: Then print the length of that list.

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch0.starterCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

# TODO: create a mixed catalog list with one Book and one Movie.
# Do not print yet.

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

# TODO: create a mixed catalog list with one Book and one Movie.
# Do not print yet.

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch0.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

catalog = [Book("The Giver"), Movie("Soul")]
print(len(catalog))

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

catalog = [Book("The Giver"), Movie("Soul")]
print(len(catalog))

0.message: Create at least one Book object.

1.message: Create at least one Movie object.

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch1.title: Loop through Book and Movie objects
try_polymorphic_collections_sketch1.prompt: Use the provided classes and mixed `catalog` list. Loop through the list and print each object's `label()` result. Do not use `if type(...)`; trust the shared method name.
try_polymorphic_collections_sketch1.hint: Use `for item in catalog:` and call `item.label()` inside the loop.

help.concept: The loop does not need to know whether the current object is a Book or Movie. It only needs the object to respond to `label()`.
help.hint_1: The loop variable can be named `item`.

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch1.starterCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

catalog = [
    Book("A Wrinkle in Time"),
    Movie("Up"),
]

# TODO: loop through catalog and print item.label() for each item.

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

catalog = [
    Book("A Wrinkle in Time"),
    Movie("Up"),
]

# TODO: loop through catalog and print item.label() for each item.

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch1.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

catalog = [Book("The Lightning Thief"), Movie("Up")]

for item in catalog:
    print(item.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

catalog = [Book("The Lightning Thief"), Movie("Up")]

for item in catalog:
    print(item.label())

0.message: Book.label() should return the expected label.

1.message: Movie.label() should return the expected label.

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch1.expectedOutput:
Book: The Lightning Thief
Movie: Up

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch2.title: Return labels from a helper
try_polymorphic_collections_sketch2.prompt: Finish the pattern by moving the loop into `services/catalog_report.py`. Complete `build_labels(items)` so it returns a list of each item's label. Then use it from `main.py`.
try_polymorphic_collections_sketch2.hint: Create an empty list, loop through `items`, append `item.label()`, and return the list.

help.concept: A helper becomes more reusable when it depends on a shared method instead of checking every exact class.
help.hint_2: Return the finished labels list from the function.

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch2.starterCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_report import build_labels

catalog = [
    Book("A Wrinkle in Time"),
    Movie("Up"),
]

# TODO: call build_labels(catalog) and print each returned label.

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_report import build_labels

catalog = [
    Book("A Wrinkle in Time"),
    Movie("Up"),
]

# TODO: call build_labels(catalog) and print each returned label.

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch2.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_report import build_labels

catalog = [Book("Charlotte's Web"), Movie("Encanto")]
for label in build_labels(catalog):
    print(label)

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_report import build_labels

catalog = [Book("Charlotte's Web"), Movie("Encanto")]
for label in build_labels(catalog):
    print(label)

0.message: Book.label() should still return the expected label.

1.message: Movie.label() should still return the expected label.

sc-polymorphism-mixed-list.title: Why the mixed list works
sc-polymorphism-mixed-list.prompt: Why can one `catalog` list hold both `Book` and `Movie` objects?
sc-polymorphism-mixed-list.hint: Think about what lists can store in Python.

help.concept: A list can store different objects, and later code can use shared behavior across them.
help.hint_1: The objects do not need to be the same subclass.
help.hint_2: The shared method name matters when the loop uses the list.

options.a: Because Python turns both objects into strings first
options.b: Because a list can store different objects together
options.c: Because Book secretly becomes Movie inside the list
options.d: Because lists only allow subclasses of the same name

fb-polymorphism-shared-call.title: Choose the shared method
fb-polymorphism-shared-call.prompt: Which shared method should be called on each item?
fb-polymorphism-shared-call.hint: Use the method name both subclasses provide.

help.concept: Polymorphism depends on one shared method name.
help.hint_1: The method returns the display text for the catalog item.

dr-polymorphism-loop-order.title: Order the loop steps
dr-polymorphism-loop-order.prompt: Put these steps in order for a polymorphic helper.
dr-polymorphism-loop-order.hint: Start with the list, then loop, then call the shared method.

help.concept: A polymorphic helper follows a simple receive-loop-call-store pattern.
help.hint_1: The list comes before the loop.
help.hint_2: The append step happens after the method returns a value.

tokens.t4: Add the returned label to the results list

sc-polymorphism-no-type-check.title: Avoid the wrong pattern
sc-polymorphism-no-type-check.prompt: Which approach best matches polymorphism in this module?
sc-polymorphism-no-type-check.hint: Pick the choice that avoids class-by-class branches.

help.concept: Polymorphism keeps later code generic by depending on shared method names.
help.hint_1: The loop should call one shared method.
help.hint_2: It should not need `if item is Book` checks.

options.a: Write a separate loop for every subclass forever
options.b: Rename the method in each subclass so they are all different
options.c: Check each object type before every method call
options.d: Call the same method on each object in the mixed list

sketch-0.title: A mixed list works when the objects share a promise
sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
Polymorphism sounds fancy, but the everyday idea is simple.

Think about a TV remote. The power button works on different TVs because every TV agrees to understand the same button.

In Python, different objects can agree to answer the same method call:

```python
items = [
    DeliveryTruck("North route"),
    DeliveryBike("Campus route"),
]
```

The objects are different kinds, but they both have `label()`. That shared method is the button the rest of the code can press.

sketch-1.title: One loop can use the shared method
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
Once every object in the list has the same method name, the loop can stay simple:

```python
for item in items:
    print(item.label())
```

The loop does not ask, "Are you a truck or a bike?" It just trusts that each object knows how to answer `label()`.

That is the heart of polymorphism: different objects, same method call, correct result for each object.

sketch-2.title: A helper can depend on the shared behavior
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
The same idea works inside a helper function:

```python
def build_labels(items):
    labels = []
    for item in items:
        labels.append(item.label())
    return labels
```

The helper does not care about the exact child class. It only cares that each object has the method it needs.

That keeps the code open to new child classes later without rewriting the loop every time.
